questions.question: What is monocropping in agricultural practices?

options.label: A
options.text: Growing one crop, then rotating to new crops

options.label: C
options.text: Growing one crop per field, rotating fields yearly

options.label: D
options.text: Cultivating a single crop species on the same land yearly

answer.label: D
answer.text: Cultivating a single crop species on the same land yearly

questions.explanation: Monocropping means cultivating the same crop species on the same field year after year. This method does not involve rotating crops. Monocropping is common in modern agriculture for crops like maize, wheat, or cotton. Continuous monocropping can lead to reduced soil fertility and increased susceptibility to pests and diseases.

questions.question: Where is the headquarters of the coconut development board located?
questions.explanation: The headquarters of coconut development board located at kochi. It is a statutory body established under the Ministry of agriculture. Coconut is a plantation crop whose development is very important for enhancing farmer’s income.

questions.question: Which country is the largest destination for Indian seafood exports?
questions.explanation: USA was the top destination for Indian seafood exports in 2024–25 with a value of USD 2.78 billion. In early FY26, US import tariffs on Indian shrimp were reduced to 18%. Non-US markets saw growth: China had a 16% export increase, Vietnam’s exports doubled to USD 0.18 billion, and Belgium’s doubled to USD 0.14 billion compared to the previous year.

questions.question: Which is India’s second largest energy source after fossil fuels?
questions.explanation: In FY 2024-25, fossil fuels contribute over 75% to India’s energy supply. As of 2025, solar power has surpassed other renewables as the leading non-fossil energy source. India added 27.20 GW of solar capacity in FY 2025-26. India’s installed solar capacity is the third largest globally, ahead of wind, hydro, and nuclear power capacities.

questions.question: What is the share of agriculture, forestry, and fishing in India’s 2024 GDP?
questions.explanation: Agriculture, livestock, forestry, and fishing contributed approximately 18% to India’s nominal Gross Value Added in 2024. The First Advance Estimates for FY 2025-26 indicate a similar proportion. The share has declined from 52% in 1951 to 16-18% in 2024. The sector employs about 45.5% of India’s workforce.

questions.question: Which among the following are the features of New Industrial policy of 1991? Public sector de-reservation Industrial licensing Liberalized Foreign Investment Policy Capital account convertibility Choose the correct option from the codes given below:
questions.explanation: The features of the New Industrial Policy, 1991 are: 1. Public sector de-reservation 2. privatization of the public sector through disinvestment. 3. Amendments to Monopolies and Restrictive Trade Practices (MRTP) Act, 1969. 4. Liberalized Foreign Investments . 5. Foreign Technology Agreements (FTA). 6. Dilution of protection to SSI and emphasis on competitiveness enhancement. The capital account convertibility was not allowed totally. The economy was opened to a certain extent but the government control still existed.

questions.question: When did India become a permanent member of the Washington Accord?
questions.explanation: The Washington Accord is an is an elite international agreement on engineering studies and the mobility of engineers. There are 20 permanent signatories to the Washington Accord. India became a permanent member of the Washington Accord (WA) in June 2014. The Washington Accord is an international accreditation agreement for undergraduate professional engineering academic degrees between the bodies responsible for accreditation in its signatory countries and regions.

questions.question: What is India’s share in global paper production?
questions.explanation: India accounts for approximately 5% of worldwide paper production as per recent industry data. India is the 15th largest paper producer globally by volume. The Indian paper industry comprises more than 800 mills. India’s total annual paper production exceeds 20 million tonnes in recent years.

questions.question: Which among the following is / are raw material(s) used for paper Industry in India? Bamboo Recycled fibre Soft wood Rice husk Choose the correct option from the choices given below:
questions.explanation: The following is the major raw material used for paper Industry in India:1. Bamboo2. Recycled fibre3. Softwood4. Rice husk5. Wheat Straw6. Bagasse

questions.question: Which is the most used raw material in India’s paper industry?
questions.explanation: Recycled fibre comprises about 71% of India’s paper production as per latest industry data. Wood and bamboo contribute approximately 21%, and agro residues 8%. The Chemical Recovery and Emission Prevention (CREP) norms accelerated the industry’s shift from wood to recycled fibre after 2003. India is among the leading global consumers of recycled fibre in paper manufacturing.
